Table 9: Regularized lid-driven cavity flow: errors and empirical rates. (a) L2 errors

Figure 8: stokes equaition when d = 2, k = 2. Top: reference Stokes velocity field u†. Middle: learned divergence-free approximations un with increasing neuron count n (left to right). Bottom: pointwise error heatmaps e(x) = ∥un(x)−u†(x)∥2. In each velocity panel, the background color encodes the speed |u(x)|, and arrows indicate the velocity direction.

Figure 9: Solving classical lid-driven cavity flow by divergence-free FNS, k = 2, n = 3202. Restricted evaluation on [−0.9, 0.9]2 shows that the large errors occur near the top two corners, especially for the Ḣ1 error.
Figure 10: Solving regularized lid-driven cavity flow by divergence-free FNS, k = 2, n = 3202. Different from Figure 9, no large error spike here due to the regularized lid.

Figure 2: Solving Stokes equation in div-free FNS: relative Ḣ1 seminorm error decay with number of neurons. (Left) d = 2. (Right) d = 3. Dashed lines in matching colors show the theoretical upper bound slopes for reference.
Table 3: Divergence-free L2 approximation in d = 3: relative L2 errors and empirical rates.
Table 2: Divergence-free L2 approximation in d = 2: relative L2 errors and empirical rates.
Table 10: Sensitivity analysis of the Stokes problem in d = 2 with k = 4. Panels (a)–(c) report the results for different boundary penalty parameters λ∂Ω.

Figure 11: Solving classical lid-driven cavity flow by divergence-free FNS compared with finite element method: L2 error and Ḣ1 seminorm error decay with degree of freedom. Left: L2 error. Right: Ḣ1 seminorm error.

Figure 5: Solving regularized lid-driven cavity flow by divergence-free FNS compared with finite element method: L2 error and Ḣ1 seminorm error decay with degree of freedom. Left: L2 error. Right: Ḣ1 seminorm error.
Figure 6: d = 2, k = 4 divergence-free L2 approximation: error decay (left) and condition number (right), comparing normal equations (mass matrix) vs. direct least squares.

Figure 1: Divergence-free L2 approximation: relative L2 error decay with number of neurons. (Left) d = 2. (Right) d = 3. Each solid curve is accompanied by a dashed line of corresponding color, whose slope equals the theoretical upper bound on the convergence rate.
Table 11: Sensitivity analysis of the Stokes problem in d = 3 with k = 4. Panels (a)–(c) report the results for different boundary penalty parameters λ∂Ω.
Table 8: Solving classical lid-driven cavity flow by divergence-free FNS: errors and empirical rates for k = 3.

Figure 7: divergence-free L2 approximation when d = 2, k = 2 Top: ground-truth divergence-free field u†. Middle: learned approximations un from three checkpoints with increasing neuron count n (left to right). Bottom: pointwise error heatmaps e(x) = ∥un(x) − u†(x)∥2 for the same three models. In each velocity panel, the background color encodes the speed |u(x)|, while arrows indicate the flow direction.
